title: Pholourie
subtitle: Trinidadian fried split pea balls
excerpt: Small, crispy-skinned balls of ground yellow split pea batter fried until golden and hollow inside, served with mango chutney or pepper sauce for dipping. Pholourie is the most popular Trinidadian street snack, sold at doubles vendors alongside the main dish, at food stalls, and made at home for parties. The split pea batter is seasoned with turmeric and cumin and has a faint nuttiness from the peas.
sourceNotes: Pholourie comes from the Indian culinary tradition of bajiyas and vadas (fried split pea fritters) adapted by the Indo-Trinidadian community. The name comes from the Hindi word for hollow bread (phulori). They are the most ubiquitous street snack in Trinidad, sold from trays alongside doubles and available at every food stall.

content.text: The split peas need to soak for at least 2 hours before grinding, longer softening produces a smoother batter and a better texture in the finished pholourie. After soaking, drain and grind the peas with only a small amount of water. The ground peas form the body of the batter; flour is added in a smaller quantity than you might expect.

Pholourie batter should be thick enough to scoop in rounded tablespoons that hold their shape when dropped into oil. If the batter is too thin, the pholourie will spread and not hollow out. If too thick, they will be dense rather than light. The batter should fall from a spoon in a thick ribbon and hold its dome shape for a second before settling.

Drain the soaked {{split-peas-yellow}} and blend with 80 ml of water to a coarse paste. Combine with the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-dried}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{garlic}}, {{scotch-bonnet}} if using, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}}. Mix well and add enough warm water (about 50 to 80 ml) to form a thick, scoopable batter. Rest for 30 minutes. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} to 170°C. Drop rounded tablespoons of batter into the oil, the pholourie should bob up and float immediately. Fry for 3 to 4 minutes, turning frequently, until deep golden all over. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve with mango chutney and pepper sauce.

items.fix: Ensure the yeast has some sugar and warm water to activate in. The pholourie hollow out from the CO2 the yeast produces during frying. A batter that has rested for 30 minutes will give better results than one fried immediately
items.cause: Too little yeast activation or batter too thick
items.symptom: Pholourie dense and heavy rather than airy

items.fix: Add flour a tablespoon at a time. The batter must be thick enough to hold a scoop shape for a second before it settles
items.symptom: Pholourie flat and spreading in the oil

items.fix: Test the oil with a small drop of batter. It should rise to the surface immediately and sizzle actively. At 170°C pholourie take exactly 3 to 4 minutes to turn deep golden

content.text: Pholourie is inseparable from the Trinidadian street-food experience. At every doubles vendor, alongside the bara and channa, a tray of pholourie will be frying. They are eaten hot, straight from the oil, dipped in mango or tamarind chutney, a handful at a time. At Trinidadian parties and home gatherings they are made in large batches as a starter while the main course finishes cooking. In Britain pholourie is one of the less-known Caribbean foods outside the community, but within the Trinidadian diaspora it is an essential party food and a taste of home.
